result:
The sound of wind through leaves is a whispered archive, a living library shelved on spires of bark and xylem. Every rustle is a syllable, every creak a footnote to an epic composed of water, sun, and patience. When I was five I believed trees could dream; I still do, only now I think their dreams outlast ours, stretching like roots under continents, drinking from fossil aquifers of loneliness no human has ever named. Under the oldest cedar in the park I once pressed my forehead to the ground and felt the earth’s pulse, slow as glaciers, convinced it was a heartbeat and not just the seismic echo of a star burning itself polite. We call it gravity, but it might be affection: the planet reaching up to keep us from drifting into the black sarcasm of space.  

Memory is a similar grasp, only smaller. We cloak it in muscle and sinew, hide it behind tongues that stumble when they try to scale the cliffs of unsaid love. Tonight I remember skipping stones with my grandfather on the Susquehanna; he told me flat ones fly farther because they apologized to the water more politely. I laughed, but I’ve spent decades practicing apologies—flattening my own sharp edges—still watching most of them sink without a ripple. Yet the river keeps its counsel. Dams rise, cities erode, entire alphabets of fish go extinct, and the Susquehanna keeps sliding the present into the Atlantic’s ancient infinity, a liquid equator between what we forget and what forgets us.  

Last week I tried to take a photograph of moonlight on the kitchen floor. Every exposure was a ghost, smeared silver, illegible. I deleted them all. It strikes me now that the failure was the fidelity; some experiences demand obsolescence. Like the lullabies my mother hummed while washing peas, pitch so soft only the steam rising off the stainless sink could carry it. Or the heat of my first lover’s palm against a bus window in February, breath fusing our hands in a fleeting aquarium. Try printing those and you’re left with condensation, with absence’s silhouette. Save them instead the way trees save drought years: thin rings, yet harder than the lush.  

Sometimes, walking pre-dawn side streets, I follow the scent of bread escaping a bakery vent. It navigates the cold better than any map. The city’s grid dissolves; I’m seven again, barefoot on linoleum, grandmother pulling a tray of bolillos from the clay oven like minor suns. The crust sang when tapped—an audible halo. What is holiness, if not bread witnessed in the dark before it belongs to anyone? Carbohydrate comet, risen and falling back into mouths, into mitochondria, into the communal ash we all become. Somewhere a cosmologist writes equations proving the universe is flat; I counter with yeast, its urgent inflation, its insistence on roundness despite every straight horizon.  

Science and superstition are neighboring nerves in the human jaw. Chew long enough and you hit both. When the dentist numbs half my face, I test language by pronouncing words like “forever” and “yes.” With the left side frozen, they exit like prisoners on parole: uncertain and grateful. I make promises anyway—yes to dinner plans, yes to a project I already regret—because to speak is to stitch a future loosely to the present with fraying thread. Meanwhile the drill sings its minor chord, reminding me enamel is just domesticated meteorite, calcium forged in stellar kilns. I wonder if a star ever grieved the fragment that became my molar.  

On the subway today a child dropped a crimson bouncy ball. It ricocheted between metal poles, defying the vacuum of commuter silence. Strangers laughed collectively, involuntary as hiccups. In that moment the train car tilted toward utopia: everyone choosing to be startled by joy rather than resentful of interruption. The child was too young to know she’d engineered communion. I store the scene like rainwater in the cistern of ordinary miracles. When headlines next inform me that the Arctic wept more ice into the sea, I’ll retrieve the bounce, the communal gasp, proof that motion and kindness remain adjacent on the periodic table of human behavior.  

Midnight, insomnia. The upstairs piano begins, hesitant, one note followed by the appointed shadow. Brahms Intermezzo, op. 118 no. 2. My ceiling becomes a resonant diaphragm, vibrating with someone else’s longing. I picture the pianist—gray-haired insomniac, maybe widowed, perhaps a teenager avoiding AP chemistry. Whoever they are, they play the way we should enter fire: gradually, bowed, but without retreat. I envy their fingers measuring grief in intervals. I think of Schumann committing himself, of Clara listening across walls thicker than empires, and I realize loneliness is just music waiting for its instrument. Downstairs I match my breathing to the tempo until both rooms orbit the same slow planet.  

Dawn arrives impersonating exhaustion. I peel an orange and the rind sprays citrus constellations across the cutting board. I taste January and a grove in Valencia simultaneously—quantum breakfast. The physicists say particles can be entangled across galaxies; breakfast was already evidence. I save one seed, wrap it in damp paper towel, slip it into a pocket as if planning a future pause in the conversation we call agriculture. Perhaps my great-grandniece will stand beneath its branches, tasting this same morning refracted through years and soil, her tongue translating stored sunlight into narrative. Between now and then will be departures enough to empty any harbor, yet the seed is a traveler unconcerned with visas.  

And time, the chronic illusionist, continues to saw us in half while we applaud the trick. We emerge on either side of the curtain believing ourselves intact. Meanwhile the cedar I hugged as a child has become a stump; memory grows concentric around the loss. I keep its fallen cone on my desk like a paperweight for unfinished poems. Each glance reminds me to be brief and aromatic, to live the way pine needles do—short-lived yet eternally pitched in the key of green. Somewhere, a stone my grandfather skipped still sleeps under river mud. Somewhere the note I failed to play recedes like the train I just missed, whistle shrinking into tomorrow. And still the wind rehearses its syllabus of leaves.
